topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope felt flimsy in my hand, the cheap paper whispering secrets I didn't want to hear. It was my essay, the one I’d poured weeks of agonizing introspection into, now sitting on a glossy page, framed by a glowing university logo. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like spilled ink. I snatched the printout, my knuckles turning white. The words, my voice, laid bare for anyone to dissect, mocked the vulnerability I’d tried so desperately to shield. I crumpled the paper, the sound sharp and unforgiving in the quiet dorm room. Every line felt like an accusation, a public dissection of my supposed depth. I shoved it under a pile of textbooks, breathing in shallow, ragged gasps, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my jaw clenched so tightly my molars ached. It was the essay—my carefully crafted narrative about overcoming adversity—now displayed in pristine, professional formatting. The sheer audacity of it made my skin crawl. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, shaky breath that tasted like ash. My gaze swept over the polished prose, cataloging every carefully chosen adjective, every perfectly structured sentence. A burning sensation rose in my chest, hot and pressurized. I reached out, my fingers trembling slightly, and deliberately dragged a nail across the printed text, just to feel some kind of physical resistance to the intrusion. This wasn't inspiration; it felt like a violation.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I didn't move immediately. My breath hitched, catching somewhere deep in my throat. It was my personal statement, circulating among prospective applicants. The sight of it—my raw, unedited thoughts presented as some kind of polished artifact—sent a wave of intense irritation through me. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to physically distance myself from the display. My hands balled into fists at my sides, the tension radiating through my arms. "This isn't fair," I muttered, the words thin and brittle, directed more at the paper than at anyone else. I needed to destroy it, to erase the evidence that I had ever been this exposed.
